lines.text: The steps of Ridgemont High rise up like a dare. Packs of students flow past in shapes that already know themselves.
Okay. New school, new me. Except it's still just me.
Great plan, Wren. Really airtight.
Find the door. Find one friendly face. That's the whole job today.
Cool, cool. Love it here already.
The first bell hasn't rung yet. The sorting already has.
Hey — you're the new one, right? Wren? I'm Jordan. Official Ridgemont Buddy, unpaid, mostly reliable.
'Mostly' is doing some heavy lifting in that sentence.
Honestly? Fair. Okay — locker's yours, two-two-six. Handle sticks if you don't lift it first. Everybody falls for it. Consider that your welcome gift.
...Wow. Incredible gift. Truly generous.
I know, I know. Save the thank-you card.
Down the hall, small clusters of students clock the new face without bothering to look away fast.
So — you wanna jump in, say hi to somebody? Or you want the rundown first, figure out who's who before you pick a lane?
lines.action: hands drawing a map in the air

lines.text: Two minutes in and already a fork in the road.
Hi — I'm Wren. Just moved here. Fair warning, I will get lost at least twice today.
A couple of kids laugh. One waves back. Easy enough.
Okay, see, that's a whole move. Most new kids just stand there like they're waiting to be adopted.
I contain multitudes.
lines.action: half a beat late, still reading the room

lines.text: Mia passes with her group, slows just enough to be noticed slowing.
Wren. Cute. Very... confident, for a first period.
We'll see if it holds up. Love that for you, though.
Honestly? That's the most attention she's paid a new kid since — actually, I don't think she's ever done that.
Great. Day one and I'm already someone's project.
Rundown me. Who am I dealing with.
Okay so. See the table by the trophy case? That's Mia's. Mia Delgado. She's — magnetic's the word people use. Also the word people use right before something goes sideways.
Noted. Approach with caution, like a vending machine that eats your money sometimes.
That's — yeah, actually, exactly that.
That's Alex. Eats lunch alone most days. Not 'cause nobody likes him, just — nobody's tried much either.
lines.action: nods toward a kid by the window, sketchbook angled away

lines.text: Filed away. Things I know are true: Mia is a vending machine. Alex eats alone.
Somebody should try.
lines.emotion: quieter, more direct — this one she actually cares about

lines.text: ...Yeah. Somebody should.
The first bell rings, sharp and sudden. The hallway starts moving toward homeroom.
Room 214. Two seats open: one at Mia's crowded table, already laughing about something. One by the window, next to Alex, who hasn't looked up from his sketchbook.
This is me bailing — good luck, rookie. You got this.
Wren! Wait — sit, sit, we're literally about to settle something very important.
lines.action: arm draped on the empty chair

lines.text: Alex doesn't look up. Doesn't call out. Just keeps drawing, like he already knows how this goes.
Two open chairs. Two whole years, maybe, riding on this.
Okay. No pressure.
Okay so first things first — where are you actually from, and please say something more interesting than 'nowhere.'
Ohio. So. Nowhere, but with worse weather.
"Worse weather." Okay, I'm obsessed, she's funny.
Not like — funny weird. Funny good. Just to be clear.
lines.action: goes very still for half a second

lines.text: Wow. Relieved.
The table laughs — with her, this time, not at her. It feels good. It also feels like a test with no visible rules.
Okay but real talk — you seem like you get it. Not everyone does, on day one.
Get what, exactly? Feels rude to ask. Feels important to find out.
Hey. Is this seat actually free or is that a trap.
No trap. Just... nobody usually asks.
Cool. I'll just sit here and be new at you, then.
That's — yeah. Okay. Fair.
lines.emotion: almost a laugh, surprised by it

lines.text: Slowly, like he's deciding something, Alex turns the sketchbook a few inches toward her.
It's dumb. It's just a robot. It doesn't really do anything.
lines.emotion: quiet, not quite meeting her eyes

lines.text: It's really good, actually.
...You didn't have to say that.
I know. Still true, though.
First real thing all day that didn't feel like a performance.
Ms. Patel reads off groups. Mia. Alex. Wren. The room shuffles chairs into a loose triangle.
Okay so we need a topic, and I vote something that isn't boring, no offense to whatever boring thing is about to get suggested.
We could do... the water cycle, but like, from the ocean's perspective? Like a story instead of just a diagram?
A beat of silence. Mia goes very still.
Wait — the ocean's "perspective"? Oh my god, that's — no, that's kind of adorable, actually. Very third grade.
A couple of kids nearby laugh too, testing whether it's safe to. Alex goes still, staring at his notebook like it might disappear him.
...Rough.
lines.action: wincing, chewing pen cap, not quite intervening

lines.text: The room's not looking at Mia right now. It's looking at me.
Actually, I think that's a really good idea. Nobody does the ocean's side of it. That's kind of the point.
lines.emotion: steady, slower than her usual nervous pace

lines.text: The room's laughter dies down, uncertain now which way to lean.
...Okay. Wow. Defensive much?
Not defensive. Just — it's a good idea. I'm allowed to say that.
Sure. Whatever. It's cute, I guess.
Mia turns back to her notebook a little too quickly, already deciding what this means about the new kid.
...You didn't have to do that.
lines.emotion: barely above a whisper, not looking at her

lines.text: I know. Still true, though.
...Thanks.
lines.action: quick, sharp eye contact, then away

lines.text: Cost something, that. Feels worth it anyway.
Okay, but what are we actually supposed to turn in — like, is there a rubric, or are we just improvising a whole ocean documentary here.
A ripple of laughter, easier this time. The tension breaks like a held breath let go.
Fine, fine, Ms. Group Project. Lead the way.
Ms. Patel starts handing out rubric sheets. The moment passes. Alex's idea doesn't come up again.
...It's fine. Rubric's probably better anyway.
Nobody got hurt. Nobody got picked either.
Hey — we can still do it your way. Just, like, with a checklist attached.
lines.action: glancing at him, half a beat too late

lines.text: ...Maybe.
lines.emotion: small, surprised, doesn't quite believe it

lines.text: Yeah, okay — very third grade.
See, SHE gets it.
Alex doesn't look up. Doesn't argue. Just goes very still, the way he does when something actually lands.
Okay, real topic, go — someone say something that isn't embarrassing.
Alex doesn't offer anything else for the rest of class. Not once.
That was easy. Weirdly, that's the part that bothers me.
...Sorry.
lines.emotion: quiet, not quite loud enough for Mia to hear

lines.text: It's fine.
It very clearly is not.
The cafeteria sorts itself the way cafeterias do — fast, and without asking permission.
Wren! Over here, we saved you a seat, obviously!
Across the room, Alex sits alone again, tray untouched, not looking over — not quite.
So. Big decision number two of the day.
lines.action: appearing at player's elbow, hands in pockets

lines.text: Cool. Love this for me.
Honestly? However you land, it says something. That's just how lunch works here.
One table's loud and easy. One table's quiet and true. Guess I pick which kind of true I want to be.
Hey. Mind some company twice in one day?
No. I mean — no, sit. Please.
A beat. Alex relaxes, just slightly, shoulders dropping half an inch.
We moved here two years ago. I thought by now I'd have, like — a table. A group. Turns out that's not really how it works.
lines.emotion: quieter, more words than he's used all day

lines.text: How does it work?
Apparently you need to talk to people. Rude, honestly.
Yeah, huge design flaw.
lines.action: half a beat late, then grins

lines.text: Alex actually laughs — short, surprised at himself.
This is — this is nice. I don't really do this. The talking-at-lunch thing.
Small table. Feels bigger than it looks.
Okay, sit, sit — we were just deciding if Mr. Alvarez is secretly twenty-five or actually forty, it's very divisive.
I'm going with time-traveling twenty-five-year-old. Controversial, I know.
The table erupts — laughing, arguing, pulling player into three conversations at once. It's loud. It's easy. It's fun, actually.
...Okay, don't tell anyone, but today's actually been kind of a good day. Don't let that get around.
Across the cafeteria, Alex glances over — just once — then looks back down at his tray.
Filed away, whether I want it there or not.
...Yeah. Good day.
lines.action: glancing back, half a beat too late

lines.text: Budge up. Bringing a friend.
lines.action: dragging a chair, casual like it's no big deal

lines.text: She sets the chair down between the two tables — close enough that it's obviously on purpose.
...Okay, that's — that's a choice.
Alex hovers behind her, tray in hand, clearly ready to bail if this goes wrong.
Oh, we're doing this. Okay. Alex, budge — c'mon, this is happening.
A beat of pure, awkward silence. Then Jordan starts talking about Mr. Alvarez's age like nothing weird is happening at all, and somehow, that's the thing that makes it normal.
...Okay, what's the robot thing you're always drawing. I've seen it like six times, I'm invested now.
lines.action: to Alex, almost surprised at herself

lines.text: It's... actually kind of a long story.
lines.emotion: startled, then carefully, a little proud

lines.text: Didn't fix everything. Just moved two chairs. Turns out that's sometimes the same thing.
The walk home cuts through the same streets, quieter now, the day settling like dust.
So. How do you think today went?
Define "went."
Honestly? However you want. I'm not grading you.
I don't know. I don't know if I'm "confident" or just really good at faking it until the bell rings.
Maybe that's the same thing, first week. Maybe it's not. Ask me in a month.
lines.action: chewing his pen cap, thoughtful for once

lines.text: One day. One version of myself, tried on in public. Doesn't have to be the only one.
Whatever happens tomorrow — I don't think I want to be a different version of me for people I just met.
Yeah. That tracks. Good. That's — yeah.
The walk home stretches ahead, familiar already after just one day.
Same person. New building. That's the whole plan, actually. Turns out that's enough.
I think surviving here means reading the room. Figuring out which version of me to lead with.
Maybe. Just — don't lose the original in all that adjusting.
Somewhere behind them, the school shrinks into an ordinary building again, no longer a whole world.
Adapt, don't disappear. Fine line. I'll find it.
Maybe I'm not really a Mia's-table person or an Alex's-table person. Maybe my thing is just... getting the tables talking.
That's a hard job. Good thing you're new — nobody's told you it's impossible yet.
The sidewalk splits ahead — Jordan's turn one way, player's the other.
Big plan, for a Tuesday. Guess I've got a whole year to figure out if it's true.

options.text: "Let's do the rundown. I like knowing what I'm walking into."

a.text: Oh, okay. No warm-up laps for you.

b.text: Smart. Okay, c'mere, let Uncle Jordan paint you a picture.

options.text: Slide into the seat at Mia's table.
Take the seat next to Alex by the window.

a.text: Okay, everyone, this is Wren, she's new, she's already better than half of you, don't ask me how I know.

b.text: ...Oh. Uh. Hey. You can — yeah. Sit wherever. I don't own the window.
b.emotion: startled, glancing up half a second

options.text: "Actually, I think that's a really good idea."
"Okay, but what are we actually supposed to turn in — let's just start there."

b.text: Fine, fine, Ms. Group Project. Lead the way.

options.text: Head over and sit with Alex.
Grab a chair and pull the two tables together.

a.action: small nod, like he clocked something

c.text: ...Oh, we're doing THAT today. Okay. Love it, honestly.

options.text: "I'm going to be myself, no matter who's watching."
"I'll figure out what each group needs from me and adapt."
"Maybe my job here is bringing people together."

a.text: Yeah. Yeah, okay. I believe that.
a.action: nods slow, like that's the answer he was hoping for

b.text: Smart. Just — don't lose track of which one's actually you. That's the only part that gets hard.

c.text: That's a hard job. Good thing you're new — nobody's told you it's impossible yet.

lines.text: Mia's still watching me like I'm a puzzle. Fine. Let her look.
You did the thing today. Walked in loud. Some people never even try that.
Yeah, well. Found out loud doesn't always mean brave. Sometimes it just means loud.
Didn't say much today. Said the right things, when it mattered.
You clocked the whole hallway before lunch. That's its own kind of brave, you know.
Guess watching counts too.
Word's already out about the chair thing. People are talking.
Good. Let them talk. Beats eating alone.
Didn't pick a side today. Just moved some furniture. Feels like it mattered more than it should.
Alex didn't say much after third period. I noticed. I didn't fix it.
You gonna talk to him? Alex, I mean.
...Yeah. Tomorrow. I mean that.
Bold got me noticed fast. Maybe next time I dial it down till I know the room.
You'll figure out when to turn the volume up. You've clearly got the volume.
It's my one setting, apparently.
You read this place like a book by second period. That's a skill, honestly.
Yeah, well. Better to know the terrain before you build on it.
Still don't know if watching first is smart or just safe. Maybe both.
Pulling those tables together — that took reading two rooms at once. That's not nothing.
Guess that's the adapting part. Figure out what each side needs, give a little of both.
Not sure it's sustainable. Worth trying again tomorrow, though.
Adapting meant going quiet when Alex needed someone loud. Noted. Filed under 'do better.'
You adapted today. Just — make sure it's not always at the same guy's expense.
...Yeah. I heard that.
You didn't exactly ease into that hallway this morning. Bridge-builder with a bit of a bulldozer streak.
I contain multitudes. Told you.
Funny — you spent all morning just watching. Now you wanna go connect everybody.
Yeah, well. Hard to build a bridge till you know both sides of the river.
You basically already did the job today. Chair-dragging and everything.
One chair. Small bridge. Still counts.
Mia and Alex, at the same table. Didn't think that'd happen on day one.
Funny thing to decide, after today. I sided with the easy table this afternoon.
You know that means going back tomorrow and actually fixing it with Alex, right?
Yeah. I know. That's kind of the point.

voiceNotes: Wren stays dry and half-a-beat-late even in narration — her INTERNAL lines should read like margin notes, not therapy-speak ('Filed away,' 'Noted,' 'Cost something, that'). She gets more direct and slows down only when something actually matters (defending Alex), which should feel like a gear-shift from her usual deflecting humor. Jordan's 'honestly' tic marks the moments he's being real instead of performing ease — use it right before any observation that actually lands. He also downplays perceptive moments with a joke immediately after ('Rough.' / half-shrug). Mia's stillness-before-the-cut is the tell for her line reads — the 'nice' version of a line should always have a half-beat pause before it, like she's aiming. Her 'literally'/'obsessed' should feel automatic, almost tic-like, not performed for effect. Alex should almost never initiate — he responds, undercuts himself, and only opens up in short bursts before catching himself. His warmth is rationed and should feel earned, not given.

alternatives: I actually really like that idea. Nobody ever does the ocean's side of it.
Wait, no, that's actually good — the ocean's-eye-view thing? That's good.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original may read slightly too composed for a nervous 13-year-old speaking up for the first time; a version with a small stumble ('Wait, no—') could better convey the effort of choosing courage over ease.

alternatives: ...Don't repeat this, but today's actually been a pretty good day.
Okay, low-key? Today's been kind of great. Don't make it weird.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Offers a slightly more clipped, teen-authentic phrasing ('low-key,' 'don't make it weird') if 'don't tell anyone' reads too formal for Mia's usual cadence.

alternatives: ...Yeah. Somebody should say hi, at least.
alternativeLines.reasoning: The original is good but slightly declarative; a version anchored to a concrete action ('sit with him') may land more naturally as something a 13-year-old would actually say rather than a thesis statement.
